REST is always faster

To manage database cursors

It includes links to related resources and available actions, allowing clients to navigate the API without hardcoded URLs
It embeds HTML pages in JSON

Atomic design decisions (colors, spacing, typography) stored as platform-agnostic variables that ensure consistency across products
Database access tokens
JavaScript authentication tokens

It manages multiple packages in one repository with intelligent caching, parallel execution, and dependency- aware task orchestration
It only manages databases
It replaces Git

intermediate.question: How do web components achieve framework-agnostic reusability?

They use browser-native APIs like Custom Elements, Shadow DOM, and HTML Templates to create encapsulated components usable in any framework
They require a special bundler plugin
They depend on a shared CSS library

Gradually integrating a framework into an existing application page by page instead of rewriting everything
Adopting only the CSS parts of a framework

options: To replace JavaScript entirely
To add static type checking to JavaScript, catching type errors at compile time and improving developer experience

Optimistic updates assume success and update the UI immediately before server confirmation, while pessimistic updates wait for the server response
Pessimistic updates never send data to servers
Optimistic updates only work with REST APIs

To provide a fast all-in-one JavaScript runtime, bundler, test runner, and package manager as an alternative to Node.js

To bundle, transform, and optimize JavaScript modules and assets for production deployment

Starting with advanced features and removing them for old browsers
Optimizing progressive image loading formats for slower connections
Restricting support exclusively to modern browsers with new features

intermediate.question: What distinguishes a Progressive Web App (PWA) from a regular web app?
options: PWAs do not use standard HTML markup or CSS styling
PWAs require React as a mandatory framework dependency
PWAs work offline, can be installed, and send notifications
PWAs only function on mobile devices and tablets

intermediate.question: Which of the following best describes the role of a web browser?
options: It stores website data on the server
It interprets and renders HTML, CSS, and JavaScript to display web pages to the user
It compiles backend code
It manages database connections
intermediate.explanation: A web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) is a client application that requests web pages from servers and renders HTML, CSS, and JS to produce the visual page the user sees.

intermediate.question: The client-server model in web development means:
options: The client and server are the same machine
The client (browser) sends requests and the server processes them and sends back responses
Only the server can initiate communication
No network is involved
intermediate.explanation: In the client-server model, the client (typically a browser) sends HTTP requests to a web server, which processes the request (possibly querying a database) and returns an HTTP response.

intermediate.question: HTTP stands for:
options: HyperText Transfer Protocol
High Transfer Text Protocol
Hyper Transfer Text Program
HyperText Transmission Process
intermediate.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the foundation of data communication on the web, defining how messages are formatted and transmitted between clients and servers.

intermediate.question: DNS (Domain Name System) is used to:
options: Style web pages
Translate human-readable domain names (e.g., google.com) into IP addresses
Compile JavaScript
Store cookies
intermediate.explanation: DNS acts as the internet's phone book, resolving domain names to IP addresses so browsers can locate and connect to the correct web server.

intermediate.question: Which HTTP status code indicates a successful response?
intermediate.explanation: 200 OK means the request was successful. 404 = Not Found, 500 = Internal Server Error, 301 = Moved Permanently (redirect).

intermediate.question: What does CSS stand for?
options: Computer Style Sheets
Creative Style System
Cascading Style Sheets
Colorful Style Sheets
intermediate.explanation: CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) controls the visual presentation of HTML elements, including layout, colors, fonts, spacing, and animations.

intermediate.question: Which HTML5 element is used to define navigation links?
options: <navigate>
<nav>
<menu>
<links>
intermediate.explanation: The <nav> element is a semantic HTML5 tag used to wrap major navigation blocks, improving accessibility and SEO.

intermediate.question: The 'alt' attribute in an tag provides:
options: A link to another image
Alternate text description for the image (used by screen readers and when the image fails to load)
Image animation settings
Image border width
intermediate.explanation: The alt attribute provides a text alternative for images, which is critical for accessibility (screen readers) and displays when the image cannot be loaded.

intermediate.question: Which HTML element is used to create a hyperlink?
options: <link>
<href>
<url>
intermediate.explanation: The (anchor) element creates hyperlinks. The href attribute specifies the URL destination. Example: Click here

intermediate.question: What is the purpose of the <meta viewport> tag in HTML?
options: To define page colors
To control the page's dimensions and scaling on different devices for responsive design
To link CSS files
To define JavaScript variables
intermediate.explanation: The viewport meta tag (e.g., <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">) tells the browser how to control the page's width and scaling on mobile devices.

intermediate.question: Which CSS property is used to change the background color of an element?
options: color
bgcolor
background-color
background-style
intermediate.explanation: The background-color property sets the background color of an element. The 'color' property sets the text color, not the background.

intermediate.question: In CSS, the 'box model' consists of:
options: Only margin and padding
Content, padding, border, and margin
Only content and border
Header, body, and footer
intermediate.explanation: The CSS box model wraps every HTML element in: Content (actual text/image), Padding (space between content and border), Border (edge around padding), and Margin (space outside the border).

intermediate.question: What does 'display: flex' do in CSS?
options: Hides the element
Enables a flexible box layout model for easy alignment and distribution of child elements
Makes text bold
Sets the element to full width only
intermediate.explanation: Flexbox (display: flex) provides a one-dimensional layout model that allows efficient distribution and alignment of items within a container, even when their sizes are unknown.

intermediate.question: A CSS media query is used to:
options: Query a database
Apply different styles based on device characteristics like screen width, height, or orientation
Import JavaScript files
Send HTTP requests
intermediate.explanation: Media queries allow responsive design by applying CSS rules conditionally based on device features. Example: @media (max-width: 768px) { ... } targets tablets and smaller screens.

intermediate.question: The CSS Grid layout is:
options: Only for images
A two-dimensional layout system for creating complex row-and-column-based layouts
A JavaScript library
A type of media query
intermediate.explanation: CSS Grid provides a two-dimensional (rows and columns) layout system. Unlike Flexbox (one-dimensional), Grid allows complex layouts with both horizontal and vertical control.

intermediate.question: Which keyword declares a block-scoped variable in modern JavaScript (ES6+)?
options: var
let
define
dim
intermediate.explanation: 'let' (and 'const') are block-scoped, meaning they exist only within the nearest curly braces {}. 'var' is function-scoped, which can lead to bugs in certain situations.

intermediate.question: What is the output of: typeof null in JavaScript?
options: "null"
"undefined"
"object"
"boolean"
intermediate.explanation: This is a well-known JavaScript quirk. typeof null returns "object" due to a legacy bug in the language's implementation that has been kept for backward compatibility.

intermediate.question: An arrow function in ES6 is defined as:
options: function => () {}
=> function() {}
arrow() => {}
intermediate.explanation: Arrow functions provide a concise syntax: (params) => { body }. They also do not have their own 'this' binding, instead inheriting it from the enclosing scope.

intermediate.question: The DOM (Document Object Model) is:
options: A CSS framework
A programming interface that represents the HTML document as a tree of objects, allowing JavaScript to manipulate page content
A database system
A server-side technology
intermediate.explanation: The DOM is a tree-structured representation of an HTML document. JavaScript can use the DOM API to read, modify, add, or delete HTML elements and attributes dynamically.

intermediate.question: A JavaScript Promise represents:
options: A guaranteed function return value
An object representing the eventual completion (or failure) of an asynchronous operation
A CSS animation
A server response header
intermediate.explanation: Promises handle asynchronous operations. They can be pending, fulfilled (resolved with a value), or rejected (failed with a reason). They enable cleaner async code than callbacks.

intermediate.question: The 'async/await' syntax in JavaScript:
options: Is used only for CSS animations
Provides a cleaner way to write asynchronous code that looks synchronous, built on top of Promises
Replaces HTML
Is only available in Node.js
intermediate.explanation: async/await lets you write Promise-based asynchronous code in a synchronous-looking style. An async function returns a Promise, and await pauses execution until the Promise resolves.

intermediate.question: In React, what hook is used to manage state in a functional component?
options: useEffect()
useState()
useRef()
useContext()
intermediate.explanation: useState() returns a state variable and a setter function. Example: const [count, setCount] = useState(0); allows the component to track and update the count value.

intermediate.question: JSON stands for:
options: JavaScript Object Naming
JavaScript Object Notation
Java Standard Object Network
JavaScript Online Notation
intermediate.explanation: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight data interchange format, easy for humans to read/write and machines to parse/generate. It is the standard format for REST API communication.

intermediate.question: In React, the Virtual DOM is:
options: A copy of the real DOM that React uses to efficiently determine what has changed and minimize actual DOM updates
A database for storing page state
A CSS rendering engine
A server-side template
intermediate.explanation: React maintains a lightweight Virtual DOM. When state changes, React compares the new Virtual DOM with the previous one (diffing), then updates only the changed parts of the real DOM (reconciliation).

intermediate.question: Angular uses which language for component logic?
options: Java
Python
TypeScript
PHP
intermediate.explanation: Angular is built with TypeScript, a typed superset of JavaScript. Components, services, and modules are written in TypeScript, which compiles to JavaScript.

intermediate.question: Node.js is:
options: A frontend CSS framework
A JavaScript runtime environment that allows running JavaScript on the server side
A relational database
An HTML preprocessor
intermediate.explanation: Node.js uses the V8 JavaScript engine (from Chrome) to execute JavaScript outside the browser, enabling server-side development with JavaScript.

intermediate.question: Express.js is:
options: A CSS framework
A minimal and flexible Node.js web application framework for building APIs and web servers
A database management system
A browser plugin
intermediate.explanation: Express.js provides routing, middleware support, and HTTP utility methods, making it the most popular framework for building REST APIs and web applications with Node.js.

intermediate.question: Which command initializes a new Node.js project?
options: npm start
npm install
npm init
node create
intermediate.explanation: npm init creates a package.json file which stores project metadata, dependencies, and scripts. It initializes a new Node.js project.

intermediate.question: REST stands for:
options: Remote Execution Standard Technology
Representational State Transfer
Reliable Server Transaction
Real-time Event Stream Transfer
intermediate.explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications. RESTful APIs use standard HTTP methods and are stateless, making them scalable and simple.

intermediate.question: Middleware in Express.js is:
options: A database query
A function that has access to the request, response, and next function, executing logic before the route handler
A type of HTML element
A CSS preprocessor
intermediate.explanation: Middleware functions can modify req/res objects, end the request-response cycle, or call next() to pass control to the next middleware. They handle tasks like logging, authentication, and parsing.

intermediate.question: Server-side rendering (SSR) means:
options: CSS is applied on the server
HTML is generated on the server for each request and sent to the client, improving initial load time and SEO
JavaScript runs only in the browser
Images are compressed on the server
intermediate.explanation: SSR generates the full HTML on the server before sending it to the browser. This improves perceived performance (faster first paint) and SEO since search engines can crawl the rendered content.

intermediate.question: SQL databases (e.g., MySQL, PostgreSQL) are:
options: Schema-less and document-oriented
Relational databases that use structured tables with rows, columns, and SQL queries
Only for file storage
Frontend technologies
intermediate.explanation: SQL (relational) databases store data in structured tables with predefined schemas, using SQL for queries. They enforce data integrity through relationships, constraints, and ACID transactions.

intermediate.question: MongoDB is an example of:
options: A relational SQL database
A NoSQL document-oriented database that stores data as JSON-like documents
A CSS framework
A web server
intermediate.explanation: MongoDB stores data as flexible BSON (binary JSON) documents in collections rather than tables. It allows dynamic schemas, making it popular for applications with evolving data structures.

intermediate.question: An ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) allows developers to:
options: Write CSS more efficiently
Interact with a relational database using programming language objects instead of raw SQL queries
Design HTML templates
Manage DNS records
intermediate.explanation: ORMs like Sequelize (Node.js), Django ORM (Python), or Hibernate (Java) map database tables to classes and rows to objects, letting developers work with databases using familiar programming constructs.

intermediate.question: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is an attack where:
options: The server crashes due to overload
An attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users
Files are deleted from the server
The database schema is modified
intermediate.explanation: XSS attacks inject malicious JavaScript into web pages. When other users view the page, the script executes in their browser, potentially stealing cookies, session tokens, or redirecting users.

intermediate.question: SQL injection can be prevented by:
options: Using inline CSS
Using parameterized queries (prepared statements) instead of string concatenation for database queries
Increasing server RAM
Disabling JavaScript
intermediate.explanation: Parameterized queries separate SQL logic from user data, preventing attackers from injecting malicious SQL code through user inputs.

intermediate.question: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) is:
options: A CSS rendering bug
An attack that tricks authenticated users into submitting unwanted requests to a web application they are logged into
A type of SQL query
A JavaScript error
intermediate.explanation: CSRF exploits the trust a web application has in an authenticated user's browser. Anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookie attributes are common defenses.

intermediate.question: JWT (JSON Web Token) is commonly used for:
options: Styling web pages
Stateless authentication by encoding user claims in a signed token sent with each request
Database queries
Image compression
intermediate.explanation: JWTs encode user identity and claims in a signed token. The server verifies the token signature on each request without needing server-side session storage, enabling stateless authentication.

intermediate.question: Lazy loading images means:
options: Loading all images immediately when the page loads
Loading images only when they are about to enter the visible viewport, reducing initial page load time
Compressing images on the server
Removing images from the page
intermediate.explanation: Lazy loading defers loading of off-screen images until the user scrolls near them. This reduces initial bandwidth, improves page load speed, and saves data for users.

intermediate.question: A CDN (Content Delivery Network) improves performance by:
options: Rewriting JavaScript code
Distributing content across geographically dispersed servers so users receive data from the nearest server
Increasing the database size
Removing CSS files
intermediate.explanation: CDNs cache static assets (images, CSS, JS) on edge servers worldwide. Users download content from the nearest edge server, reducing latency and improving load times.

intermediate.question: Code minification is the process of:
options: Adding comments to code
Removing unnecessary characters (whitespace, comments, long variable names) from code to reduce file size
Encrypting the source code
Converting code to a different language
intermediate.explanation: Minification reduces file sizes (JS, CSS, HTML) by removing whitespace, comments, and shortening variable names without changing functionality, resulting in faster downloads.

intermediate.question: Browser Developer Tools (DevTools) allow developers to:
options: Only view the page source
Inspect HTML/CSS, debug JavaScript, monitor network requests, and analyze performance in real time
Only edit images
Send emails
intermediate.explanation: DevTools (F12 in most browsers) provide panels for element inspection, console (JS debugging), network monitoring, performance profiling, and storage management.

intermediate.question: End-to-end (E2E) testing in web development:
options: Tests only CSS styling
Simulates real user interactions to test the entire application flow from frontend to backend
Tests only the database
Is the same as unit testing
intermediate.explanation: E2E tests (using tools like Cypress or Selenium) automate browser interactions to verify that the complete application works correctly from the user's perspective.

intermediate.question: Which tool is commonly used for unit testing JavaScript code?
options: Photoshop
Jest
Nginx
MongoDB
intermediate.explanation: Jest is a popular JavaScript testing framework (developed by Meta) widely used for unit testing React applications and Node.js code. It provides assertions, mocking, and code coverage out of the box.

intermediate.question: CI/CD stands for:
options: Computer Interface / Computer Design
Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment (or Delivery)
Code Inspection / Code Debugging
Client Interface / Client Database
intermediate.explanation: CI automatically builds and tests code on every commit. CD automates deployment to staging or production environments, enabling rapid and reliable software delivery.

intermediate.question: Docker containers are used in web deployment to:
options: Replace CSS frameworks
Package an application with all its dependencies into a portable, consistent environment that runs anywhere
Design web pages
Write SQL queries
intermediate.explanation: Docker containers encapsulate an application and its environment (runtime, libraries, configs), ensuring consistent behavior across development, testing, and production environments.

intermediate.question: A reverse proxy (e.g., Nginx) in front of a web application:
options: Only serves static files
Forwards client requests to backend servers, handles load balancing, SSL termination, and caching
Replaces the database
Is a type of CSS preprocessor
intermediate.explanation: A reverse proxy sits between clients and backend servers, distributing traffic, caching responses, terminating SSL, and providing an additional security layer.

intermediate.question: A RESTful API uses standard HTTP methods. Which method is used to retrieve a resource?
options: POST
PUT
GET
DELETE
intermediate.explanation: REST maps CRUD operations to HTTP methods: GET (Read), POST (Create), PUT/PATCH (Update), DELETE (Delete). GET requests should be safe and idempotent.

intermediate.question: GraphQL differs from REST in that:
options: It only works with SQL databases
Clients can request exactly the data they need in a single query, avoiding over-fetching and under-fetching
It does not use HTTP
It only supports GET requests
intermediate.explanation: GraphQL is a query language for APIs that lets clients specify exactly what data they need. Unlike REST (which returns fixed structures per endpoint), GraphQL avoids over-fetching and reduces multiple round trips.

intermediate.question: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a mechanism that:
options: Compresses API responses
Allows or restricts web applications running on one origin to request resources from a different origin
Encrypts API data
Manages database connections
intermediate.explanation: CORS uses HTTP headers to tell browsers whether to allow a web page from one origin to access resources from a different origin. Without proper CORS headers, browsers block cross-origin requests for security.

intermediate.question: A webhook is:
options: A JavaScript hook function
A user-defined HTTP callback that is triggered by a specific event, allowing real-time data push from one application to another
A CSS animation technique
A database trigger
intermediate.explanation: Webhooks enable real-time communication between services. When an event occurs (e.g., payment completed), the source application sends an HTTP POST to a URL configured by the receiving application.

intermediate.question: A Progressive Web App (PW
options: is: A) A native mobile app built with Swift
A web application that uses modern web technologies to deliver app-like experiences including offline support, push notifications, and installability
A desktop application
A database management tool
intermediate.explanation: PWAs use service workers for offline caching, web app manifests for installability, and responsive design to provide native-app-like experiences through the browser.

intermediate.question: Webpack is:
options: A CSS framework
A module bundler that packages JavaScript, CSS, and other assets into optimized bundles for production
A web server
A database tool
intermediate.explanation: Webpack bundles modules and assets (JS, CSS, images) into optimized output files. It supports code splitting, tree shaking, and hot module replacement for efficient development.

intermediate.question: Semantic versioning (SemVer) format MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH means:
options: The date the software was released
MAJOR = breaking changes, MINOR = new features (backward-compatible), PATCH = bug fixes
The number of files, functions, and tests
The team size, sprint number, and release count
intermediate.explanation: SemVer (e.g., 2.4.1) communicates the nature of changes: incrementing MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for backward-compatible features, and PATCH for backward-compatible fixes.

To store custom data accessible via JavaScript

expert.question: What is the Shadow DOM?

An encapsulated DOM tree that isolates styles
A dark mode feature provided by CSS natively

options: To create reusable email template layouts
To define HTML content not rendered until activated
To create reusable CSS template style blocks
To import external HTML files into a document

edit='true'
writable='true'

To add a link to the image source code

A server-side rendering technique for pages
